feed.title: Combined summary - Compact Isogeny PQC can replace HD wallets, key-tweaking, silent payments

entry.title: Combined summary - Compact Isogeny PQC can replace HD wallets, key-tweaking, silent payments
entry.summary: The exploration of post-quantum cryptography has highlighted isogeny-based cryptographic methods as a significant advancement, offering a more space-efficient solution for key and signature sizes compared to lattice-based schemes. These methods leverage the mapping of points between different elliptic curves through functions known as isogenies, presenting a compact alternative typically requiring less than 300 bytes for keys and signatures. The research aims to make isogeny crypto accessible to bitcoin engineers, drawing parallels with classical Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) to facilitate understanding and adoption. Isogenies serve as mappings between elliptic curves, whereas endomorphisms are specific isogenies mapping points on the same curve. Challenges such as the supersingular isogeny path problem (SIPP) and the endomorphism ring problem (ERP) underline the complexity within this field, yet also underscore its potential for creating secure cryptographic keys resistant to quantum attacks. Isogeny-based cryptography's practical applications extend across various domains, including signature schemes and key derivation methods, reflecting its potential in enhancing bitcoin's security infrastructure. Despite the progress, the development of multisignature isogeny schemes that match the compactness of existing methods remains an area ripe for exploration. Engaging the broader community of bitcoin developers in this endeavor is crucial for leveraging collective expertise and advancing the field further. The discussion also delves into the construction of zero-knowledge proofs of knowledge (zkpok) under cryptographic protocols, highlighting the transition from elliptic curve cryptography to isogeny-based systems. The foundational concept relies on one-way mappings with homomorphic properties between groups, facilitating the creation of zkpok by binding secrets to challenge values. However, achieving true zero-knowledge requires overcoming inherent limitations, which Pedersen commitments exemplify through their structure and properties. The shift towards isogeny-based systems introduces nuanced differences, like the concept of "duals" in isogenies, offering an analogous mechanism to inverses in traditional cryptography. This transition underscores the importance of evaluating the properties of post-quantum cryptographic primitives, focusing on attributes like 'rerandomization,' 'aggregatability,' and 'batch verifiability' for future-proofing security protocols against quantum-enabled attackers. The industry's adoption challenges, especially when compared to the established presence of elliptic curve cryptography, necessitate a holistic assessment of practical support and experience in integrating advanced cryptographic techniques. This comprehensive approach is vital in the evolution of cryptographic standards, preparing for post-quantum scenarios while ensuring the seamless integration and functionality of new cryptographic methods.
